findings.evidence: `JHolomorphicProd.lean:110-137` provides the full `prodMk` quartet including `IsJHolomorphicWithinAt.prodMk`; this file has `prodMap` only at lines 159, 167, and 176.
findings.fix: Add `IsJHolomorphicWithinAt.prodMap` for `hf : IsJHolomorphicWithinAt J₁ K₁ f s p.1` and `hg : IsJHolomorphicWithinAt J₂ K₂ g t p.2`, proving holomorphicity within `s ×ˢ t` at `p`, and derive the `On` lemma from it.
findings.issue: `IsJHolomorphicOn.prodMap` is added without the corresponding `IsJHolomorphicWithinAt.prodMap`, breaking the established At/WithinAt/On/global API level for product calculus.

findings.evidence: The proof already calls the general chain rule `IsJHolomorphicWithinAt.comp`, whose signature at `JHolomorphic.lean:258-263` accepts arbitrary sets plus `Set.MapsTo`; lines 128-148 specialize that to `s ×ˢ t`.
findings.fix: Generalize to `{u : Set (V × W)} {s : Set V}` with `hf : IsJHolomorphicWithinAt (J₁.prod J₂) K f u (v,w)` and `Set.MapsTo (fun v' => (v',w)) s u`; similarly for the right coordinate. The rectangular versions can be derived as corollaries if desired.
findings.issue: The within-set coordinate restriction lemmas are specialized to rectangular sets `s ×ˢ t` and require `w ∈ t`/`v ∈ s`, although the natural chain-rule form only needs a maps-to hypothesis into an arbitrary source set.

summary: The product-map calculus is under-general at the within-set level: it skips the natural `WithinAt.prodMap` API and hard-codes coordinate restrictions to rectangular source sets.
